planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; South African geranium grows from a stout, woody, tuberous root system that survives moderate drought. In zone 9 it can remain in the ground as a perennial. The velvety, silver-gray to blue-gray leaves and dark red-maroon flowers are distinctive. The roots are the source of the commercially significant herbal supplement EPs 7630 / Umcka, widely sold for upper respiratory support.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; South African geranium is a low-growing pelargonium from the semi-arid grasslands and rocky hillsides of South Africa that has achieved global commercial significance as a medicinal herb: the dried, powdered root extract — sold under the names Umcka, Umckaloabo, and EPs 7630 — is one of the best-studied herbal supplements for upper respiratory tract infections, with multiple clinical trials supporting its efficacy for reducing the duration and severity of acute bronchitis and the common cold. As a garden plant, independent of its medicinal significance, it is quietly attractive: the velvety, rounded, silver-gray to blue-gray leaves with deeply scalloped edges form a soft, spreading, woolly-textured mound, and above this cloud of silver foliage appear, in late spring through summer, clusters of small, dark, wine-red to almost-black maroon, five-petaled flowers — one of the most unusually dark-colored flowers of any pelargonium. The combination of silver foliage and very dark maroon-purple flowers is sophisticated and distinctive. It grows from a thick, tuberous, carrot-like root that survives significant drought and moderate frost. An interesting, medicinally significant herb for the zone 9 herb garden.

planting.note: Plant the large, flattened, turnip-like tuber with its top just below the soil surface in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; night-scented pelargonium follows the Cape winter-rainfall cycle — growing and flowering in winter and spring, going completely summer dormant. Keep bone dry in summer dormancy. This species holds the botanical distinction of being the first pelargonium introduced to European horticulture — brought to Leiden Botanic Garden in 1601.

directions: Plant tuber near the soil surface in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy soil in full sun; night-scented pelargonium holds a unique place in botanical history as the first pelargonium species to reach European gardens — introduced to Leiden Botanic Garden in 1601 from the Cape of Good Hope, marking the beginning of the enormously influential European fascination with South African Pelargonium species that eventually produced the entire zonal and regal geranium garden industry. The plant itself is modest but charming: a stout, flattened, turnip-shaped tuber produces in winter and spring a rosette of finely-divided, almost fern-like, deeply-pinnate, dark green leaves of considerable delicacy, and small clusters of typical pelargonium flowers in dull yellow-brown to purplish-brown — not showy in daylight, which gave rise to the species epithet 'triste' (sad, dull) and the common name 'Sad Geranium'. But in the evening, the flowers emit a powerfully sweet, vanilla-like fragrance that explains why this modest-looking plant attracted such attention from seventeenth-century botanists. It is a nocturnal moth-pollinated species, scentless by day, intensely fragrant after dark. A plant of profound historical and botanical significance and unusual sensory quality.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; fragrant tulbaghia is winter-blooming in South Africa (May–September) and produces its beautiful white flowers when little else is blooming. Unlike the violet-flowered T. violacea (Society Garlic), T. simmleri has no garlic odor — the flowers are pleasantly sweet-fragrant instead. More frost-tender than T. violacea.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; fragrant tulbaghia differs from the much more commonly grown Society Garlic (T. violacea) in three important ways: the flowers are pure white rather than violet, the flowers are pleasantly sweet-fragrant rather than garlicky-scented, and it blooms in winter rather than summer. In zone 9 gardens, this combination of white winter flowers and sweet fragrance (rather than the pungent garlic smell that deters some gardeners from T. violacea) makes it a more refined and widely appealing garden plant for positions near paths, entrances, or seating areas where the winter fragrance can be appreciated. The umbels of clean, white, six-petaled, tubular flowers on tall stems above the narrow, strap-shaped, gray-green leaves are elegant and refined; the sweet, light fragrance is detectable throughout the garden from a single established clump. Like T. violacea, it forms steadily expanding clumps that can be divided regularly. Outstanding for zone 9 winter-interest plantings where winter color and fragrance in the garden are otherwise limited.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just above the soil surface in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; yellow fire lily is a rare yellow-flowered Cyrtanthus species from the Drakensberg grasslands that blooms in summer, triggered by fire or drought stress in nature. Unlike the scarlet-red C. elatus (Scarborough Lily), this species produces clusters of small, bright-yellow, tubular flowers in summer umbels. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant bulbs in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; yellow fire lily fills an unusual niche in the Cyrtanthus genus — yellow-flowered fire lilies are distinctly uncommon compared to the dominant red, orange, and pink species. C. breviflorus produces in summer compact umbels of 3–8 small, bright yellow, nodding, tubular flowers on stems 12–18 inches tall above narrow, strap-shaped, deciduous leaves that die back in winter. The short, wide flower tubes (giving the species epithet 'breviflorus', short-flowered) are bright, clear yellow — a warm, clean, sunflower-yellow tone that stands out distinctly from the red and orange fire lilies. It comes from the summer-rainfall grasslands of the Drakensberg and Natal midlands in South Africa, where it flowers in summer following fires that burn the dry winter grass — a characteristic fire-adapted ecology shared by many Cyrtanthus species (which collectively are called 'fire lilies' for this flower-triggering behavior). In the garden it flowers reliably in summer even without fire stimulus when provided with a dry period in late winter and spring followed by watering as temperatures rise. An unusual yellow flowering option for the zone 9 summer bulb garden.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Comins's tulbaghia is a compact, smaller species than T. violacea — ideal for rock gardens, pot edges, and small spaces — that produces fragrant, white flowers. The flowers are sweetly scented rather than garlicky. More tender than T. violacea; in zone 9a it benefits from a sheltered position or container culture for winter protection.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; miniature society garlic is a charming, compact South African Tulbaghia from the rocky grasslands of KwaZulu-Natal and the Eastern Cape — smaller in all its parts than the familiar T. violacea but similarly clump-forming and similarly prolific in flower production. The small, white, sweetly fragrant (not garlic-scented) flowers are borne in compact, rounded umbels on stems only 8–14 inches tall, above very narrow, grass-like, blue-green leaves that give the plant a delicate, fine-textured appearance well suited to pot edges, rock garden plantings, and small-space gardens where T. violacea's larger clumps would be overwhelming. The sweet flower fragrance (vs the distinctive garlic odor of T. violacea) makes it preferable for positions near paths and sitting areas where one passes close. It flowers over an extended period in late spring through summer. A compact, refined alternative to Society Garlic for the smaller-scale zone 9 garden or for container culture on a shaded terrace.
